Advanced Typography
The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
Pack my box with five dozen liquor jugs.
Sphinx of black quartz, judge my vow.
How vexingly quick daft zebras jump.
fi fl ff ffi — 1234567890
Line Spacing 1.5
Blank Line Height 1 em
Height of empty lines in Read & Revise mode. Larger values add space between paragraphs. Does not affect spacing within wrapped text. Note: this setting is suspended while Ruled Lines or Dot Grid is active — blank lines are forced to match the ruled line spacing so they stay on the grid.
Letter Spacing Default
Word Spacing Default
Number Style
Controls how digits are drawn. Tabular gives equal-width digits ideal for lists and dates.
Disable Ligatures
Recommended for dyslexic readers and young students. Prevents letters from joining (e.g. fi, fl, ff).
Colors
...
Light Mode ☀️
Background
#f5f0e8
Text Color
#2a2520
Note preview color
Color of the preview text shown below note names in the list. Useful for low-vision users who need higher contrast.
#6b6258
Note texture
Subtle paper texture behind note content. Per-note themes can override.
Dark Mode 🌙
Background
#1e1c1a
Text Color
#f0ebe0
Note preview color
Color of the preview text in the dark-mode notes list. Increase for better contrast.
#b8b0a0
Note texture
Lighter texture colors for dark backgrounds
Accent Color
Accent Color
Used for buttons, toggles, and highlights
#5b7c6e
Highlight Color
Highlight Color
Color used when highlighting lines with the == marker or Highlight toolbar button
#ffe066
The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
Color-Coding Palette
Default checkbox color. Also used by the Color toolbar button for color-coding text with tags like {red}, {gold}, {rose} etc.
#888888
Custom Colors
Three custom colors for use in notes with {custom}, {custom2}, and {custom3} tags. Tap each button to change the color.
{custom}
{custom2}
{custom3}
Note Display
...
Note editing mode
Choose which editing modes are available. Disabling one mode simplifies the interface.
Convert tabs to spaces on paste
When pasting text, replace tab characters with spaces (using the indent size setting). Turn off to preserve tab characters.
Swipe right from left edge to go back
While viewing a note, swipe in from the far left edge of the screen to return to the notes list — the same gesture many other apps support. Turn off if this triggers by accident.
Show toolbar collapse button
Shows a small chevron pill above the lower toolbar that you can tap to hide or show the toolbar. Turn off if users accidentally collapse the toolbar.
Tag scope
Controls whether a tag on a parent line applies to its indented children. "Block" = inherited. "Line only" = direct tag only. Also fixes filter issues with tag-list lines at left margin.
Color-code lines by tag
In Read & Revise mode, adds a colored left border to lines based on their @tag. Each unique tag gets a different color automatically. Useful for priority tagging (e.g. @prio1 through @prio8).
Color-code tag pills by tag
In Read & Revise mode, colors each @tag pill based on tag identity using the same color assignment as the left border feature. Can be used together with or instead of the left border.
Wrap long note names
Show note names on up to 3 lines instead of cutting them off with … Useful with larger font sizes. When on, the note preview also wraps to 3 lines.
Show note preview
Show the first line of note content below the note name in the list. Turn off for a compact, name-only list.
Open notes in Read & Revise mode
When opening an existing note with text, show it in Read & Revise mode with clickable links. New and empty notes always open in Typing mode.
Large deletion warning
Ask for confirmation when you delete more than this many characters at once. Set to 0 to disable.
Show Oops restore button
Show the "Oops! Restore note" button at the bottom of the note screen.
Double-tap to edit
In Read & Revise mode, double-tap any line to switch to Typing mode with the cursor placed where you tapped.
+ button inserts at
When you tap + to quickly add text, insert it at the beginning or end of the note.
Keep quiet mode between notes
When on, quiet mode stays active when you go back and open another note. When off (default), quiet mode is always cleared when you leave a note.
Show floating + button in editor
Shows a floating + button at the lower right of the note editor screen. Tap it to quickly add text. Hidden in quiet mode and when the keyboard is open.
Show @tags in Read & Revise mode
Display @tagname labels as small gray pills in Read & Revise mode. You can also toggle this from the More menu or the Filter by tag sheet.
Collapsible outline (Read & Revise mode)
Shows a chevron next to checkbox lines that have indented children. Tap the chevron in Read & Revise mode to collapse or expand those children. Collapsed state resets when you leave the note.
Collapse all sections on open
When outline mode is on, collapse all sections each time the note is opened.
Paragraph rules (Read & Revise mode)
Show a subtle horizontal line between paragraphs in Read & Revise mode. A paragraph boundary is a non-blank line followed by one or more blank lines.
Spaces per indent / unindent
Number of spaces added or removed when you press Tab / Shift+Tab, or tap the Indent / Unindent buttons.
Layout & Margins
...
Add extra space to the left or right edges. Useful on wide screens to center content. Does not affect toolbars.
Indent width in Read & Revise mode 1.7 em
How much each indent level is indented in Read & Revise mode. Larger values make nested lists more dramatic. Does not change note text.
Indent headings by level (R&R mode)
H1 stays flush left; H2 indents by one step, H3 by two, and so on. Uses the indent width value above.
List continuation indent
In Read & Revise mode, wrapped lines of a checkbox or hyphen item align with the text after the marker, not the left edge. Makes long list items easier to read.
Paragraph continuation indent
Same idea, but for plain paragraphs (not checkbox or hyphen items). When on, extra spaces or tabs typed at the start of a line make wrapped lines align with that text too. When off (default), the spaces just indent the first line, like a regular paragraph indent.
Maximum characters per line
Off
Limits how wide each line of text can be. Uses the ch unit (width of one character). 0 = unlimited. Recommended: 60–80 for comfortable reading.
Note Content
Left margin (note editor & title)
0px
Right margin (note editor & title)
0px
Notes List
Left margin (home screen list)
0px
Right margin (home screen list)
0px
Lower Toolbar Buttons ...
Hidden buttons move to the ⋯ More menu. The + button is always visible in the lower toolbar (the More button now lives in the top toolbar). Items marked T are toolbar-only — hiding one removes it entirely instead of moving it to the More menu, since it needs a second tap on a note line right after to do anything (e.g. Check State).
Speech
...
Voice
Speech Speed
0.9×
Speak on Open
Automatically speak a note when you open it
Pause between lines
Extra pause between spoken lines (0 = no pause)
0.4s
Show Speak button
Show the Speak button in the note editor toolbar
Show Speak button in quiet mode
When on, the Speak button remains visible in quiet mode so you can still have your note read aloud.
Speak button always reads the whole note
When on, tapping Speak always reads the entire note. Accidental word selections from tapping or dragging are ignored. Selections made with the Select Lines tool are still respected.
Speak button: icon only
Hide the "Speak" label, show only the speaker icon
Speak button: accent color
Use the accent color on the Speak button. Turn off for a neutral style.
Skip list markers and formatting when speaking
When on, checkboxes - [ ] - [x], leading markers (-, *, +), and bullet characters are silently skipped. When off, the voice may say "semicolon" or "comma" for these characters.
Reading Ruler
...
Show ruler toggle in More menu
Adds a Reading ruler on/off item to the note editor More menu.
Preview
The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog
Pack my box with five dozen liquor jugs
How vexingly quick daft zebras jump
Ruler Color
#808080
Transparency 60%
0% = fully opaque, 100% = invisible
Thickness 6px
Ruled Lines & Grid
...
Ruled lines / grid
A decorative paper-style background, in both Typing and Read & Revise mode — never changes your note's actual text. Works with whatever background color you've already chosen. When active, the Blank Line Height setting (in Text) is overridden so blank lines stay on the ruled grid.
Line color
Auto: dark lines on a light background, light lines on a dark background.
Line position -5px
Dot grid position -14.5px
Checkbox-line position (independent) -1px
Margin line color (Legal pad)
#c83030
Margin line position 0px
Shift the double margin lines left or right as a unit. Positive values move them right — useful on larger screens or when using a wider left margin.
View Only Notes
...
When on, every note opens in read-only mode. Individual notes can also be marked view only from the notes list (long-press a note).
Checklists
...
Confetti on completing a checklist
Show a confetti animation when you check off the last remaining unchecked item in a note.
Move completed items to bottom
When you check off a checkbox or strike a hyphen item in Read & Revise mode, it moves to the bottom of the note.
Confirm before moving
Ask before moving a completed item to the bottom.
Default indent level for new items
Number of indent levels to apply when adding new checkbox or hyphen items via Quick Add, if smart indent doesn't apply. 0 = no indent. Uses the "Spaces per indent" setting to compute spaces.
Today note suffix
Notes with this suffix in the name get a [last modified] timestamp and a Clear button in Read & Revise mode.
Last note suffix
The backup note saved when you tap Clear is named with this suffix.
Time format
Format for the [last modified] timestamp.
Strike through text when checked
When you tap a checkbox in Read & Revise mode to check it, automatically strike through the line text.
Play a sound when checking off an item
A short sound plays when you tap a single checkbox or hyphen item and it becomes checked. Never plays when unchecking, or when checking off several items at once.
Check-off sound
Tap a name to hear and select it.
Double-space smart indent items
When typing Enter after a checkbox or hyphen item, inserts a blank line before the next item.
List item rules (Read & Revise mode)
Show a subtle horizontal line below each checkbox or hyphen list item in Read & Revise mode.
Rule above checkbox sections
Also show a rule above a checkbox item when it follows a blank line (section separator).
Bullets for hyphens (Read & Revise mode)
Display leading hyphens ( - ) as bullet points (•) in View mode. The note text is unchanged. (Bullets starting with * or + always display as bullet points, regardless of this setting.)
Multi-level bullet styles
Cycle bullet style by indent level: • ◦ ▪ (repeating), like Word.
Themes
...
Save groups of settings as named themes. Apply a theme globally or assign it to an individual note.
No themes saved yet.
Show theme indicator in note
Show a small pill near the note title when a per-note theme is active.
Debugging
...
Log actions for debugging
Records every toolbar button, More menu item, settings change, note open/exit, and confirmation dialog choice to a hidden note, so problems that are hard to reproduce can be traced afterward.
Keeps the most recent 1000 entries. Excluded from the main note list, search, and "Export all notes" — open it below to read or export it individually.

Editing a single line or paragraph — line breaks are not allowed.

Download all notes as an OPML file (for WorkFlowy, Dynalist, OmniOutliner, and others)

From a TickTick CSV backup file (Settings → Account → Generate Backup)

Help

No help topics match your search.
0 of 0
Your notes are private

All your notes are stored only on your device — they are never sent to any server or cloud service. This also means your notes stay with the device and browser you use. Use Export to back up your notes to a file.

Text too large or too small?
Tap the ⚙️ gear icon to open Settings. Under Text, you can adjust the UI Font Size (controls and labels) and the Note Text Size (note content) independently.
Share this app with a friend

Send someone this link and they can use Remember too — no install needed, it works right in their browser. Or they can add the app to their home screen using the instructions in the Optional Set Up Steps section below.

https://lynn-edu-apps.github.io/everyday-apps/remember/
About notes and formatting (formatting is optional)

Remember stores your notes as plain text — the same simple format used by basic text editors. You can write and read notes in plain text alone, and that works perfectly well.

Security tip: Do not store passwords in any plain text app, including this one. Use a password manager that encrypts them — Bitwarden and Proton Pass are good free options.

Optionally, you can switch to Read & Revise mode (tap the pencil icon in the top toolbar) to see light formatting applied to your notes: bold, italic, strikethrough, colors, and checkboxes. In View mode, checkboxes appear as real tappable boxes you can check and uncheck — not just - [ ] characters.

To add formatting, Remember uses small special characters called Markdown that you type directly into your note. For example, typing **word** makes word appear bold in Read & Revise mode. You never have to use any of this — plain text notes work just as well, and the Speak button reads both kinds naturally.

Markdown & AI tool compatibility (for the curious)

The special characters Remember uses for formatting — - [ ] checkboxes, - lists, # headings, **bold** — are not something unique to this app. They are part of an industry-wide standard called Markdown.

Markdown was invented in 2004 and is now used by GitHub, Reddit, Notion, Obsidian, WhatsApp, Slack, and many other apps. Because it is plain text, it is universally readable and never gets locked into any one program.

Quick Markdown reference

These are the standard Markdown patterns Remember supports:

What you type What it looks like
- [ ] Buy milk ☐ Buy milk (tappable checkbox)
- [x] Call dentist ☑ Call dentist (checked)
- Item one • Item one (bullet list)
# Big heading Big heading (H1)
## Smaller heading Smaller heading (H2)
**bold text** bold text
~~strikethrough~~ strikethrough
`inline code` inline code
- - -
(dividing line)

Code blocks: put a line of three backticks (```) before and after a block of text to display it in a monospace, never-formatted block — checkboxes, headings, tags, and bold/italic all stay as literal text inside one. While typing, the Code block button in the lower toolbar inserts a fence for you, wrapping any text you've already selected. Pasted code that uses indentation instead of backtick fences (a style some other apps use) won't automatically become a code block here — select it and tap the Code block button to fence it yourself.

Indentation for outlines: indent any line with spaces (or Tab) to make it a child of the line above. Enable Collapsible outline in Settings to collapse and expand nested sections.

Compatibility with AI writing tools

Because Remember uses standard Markdown, notes you write here are directly compatible with AI tools. If you ask Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, or any other AI assistant to create a task list or outline, it will naturally produce - [ ] checkboxes, - bullet points, and # headings — all of which Remember will display correctly.

Browser-integrated AI tools work the same way:

  • Apple Intelligence (iOS/macOS Writing Tools) — rewrites selected text; output uses standard Markdown
  • Brave Leo — Claude-powered assistant built into Brave browser; generates standard Markdown natively
  • Chrome AI features (Android/desktop) — passes text to Google AI; produces compatible Markdown

One thing to watch: if an AI tool rewrites or reorganizes your note, it may normalize the indentation (changing 4 spaces to 2, for example). This could affect collapsible outline hierarchies but won't break any other formatting.

Note: Remember's colored checkbox variants like [x:red] are unique to this app and won't be generated by AI tools — but that's fine, they're an optional extra.

Collapsible outline (Read & Revise mode only)

When Collapsible outline is turned on in Settings → Note Display, a small ∨ chevron appears to the left of any checkbox or hyphen line that has indented children beneath it. Tap the chevron to collapse or expand those children.

How to use the chevrons

The chevron appears to the left of the parent line — not on the checkbox or hyphen itself.

∨ (pointing down) means the section is expanded. Tap to collapse it.

› (pointing right) means the section is collapsed. Tap to expand it.

A small badge like › 5 hidden appears after the parent line text when children are hidden, so you always know content is there.

Collapsed state is session only — when you leave the note and return, everything is expanded again.

Checking all sub-items (touch)

With Collapsible outline turned on, you can long-press the ∨ chevron on any checkbox or hyphen parent item to check or uncheck all its sub-items at once.

A small menu appears with two options: Check all sub-items and Uncheck all sub-items. This applies recursively to all descendants.

This only appears for checkbox and hyphen items that have descendant checkbox items. It does not appear on heading lines.

Keyboard & mouse shortcuts (desktop)

When using a mouse, you can hold modifier keys while clicking a chevron for more powerful actions:

Action What it does
Click chevron Collapse or expand that parent only
Alt + click chevron Collapse or expand recursively — applies to all nested sub-parents too
Alt + Shift + click chevron Toggle the parent’s check state, then apply that same state to all descendants

Important: for Alt+Shift+Click, click the chevron (∨), not the checkbox square. Clicking the checkbox square will only toggle that one item.

Outline Actions sheet

Tap Outline actions… in the More (…) menu (Read & Revise mode only) for a sheet with bulk controls. Drag the handle at the top to make it taller.

Expand all / Collapse all — applies to every parent in the note at once.

Recursive toggle — when on, Expand/Collapse also drills into children of children. When off, only the immediate children of each parent are affected.

Per-parent rows — each collapsible parent is listed with four buttons:

  • Expand / Collapse — expand or collapse that specific parent (recursive setting applies)
  • ☑ All — check the parent and every checkbox or hyphen child beneath it (always recursive)
  • ☐ All — uncheck the parent and every checkbox or hyphen child beneath it (always recursive)

Checking a hyphen item (- text) applies strikethrough. Unchecking removes the strikethrough.

Tap the ? button in the sheet header for a quick reminder of what each button does.

Tips for getting started
The notes list

The notes list is the first screen you see when you open Remember. Each note shows its title and a short preview of its content.

Tap any note to open it. Tap the + button to create a new note.

To delete a note, open it and scroll all the way to the right in the lower toolbar — the trash icon is at the far right.

Use the Import / Export button on the notes list screen to back up all your notes to a file, or to restore them on a new device.

The note editor

When you open a note you start in Typing mode — you can tap anywhere in the text to position your cursor and start typing.

Tap the pencil icon (top right of the top toolbar) to switch to Read & Revise mode, which shows your note with formatting applied — bold, strikethrough, colors, and checkboxes all appear as they should.

The Speak button reads your note aloud and is available in any mode. It works best in Read & Revise mode — the text is spoken more naturally because formatting markers like bold and color have already been applied and are not read aloud.

To rename a note, tap its title at the top of the note.

The + button in the lower toolbar lets you quickly add text to the end (or beginning) of a note without tapping into the note body.

Scrolling the lower toolbar

The lower toolbar has more buttons than can fit on screen at once. You can scroll it left and right with your finger — just swipe horizontally on the toolbar itself.

Scroll arrows ( and ) appear at the edges of the toolbar when there are hidden buttons in that direction. Tap an arrow to scroll one step, or swipe the toolbar directly.

Less-used buttons like Print, Export, and Delete note are at the far right — scroll to reach them.

Optional setup steps

Everything in this section is completely optional. Remember works perfectly well without any of it — just open the link in your browser and start writing.

Installing on your Home Screen

On iPhone or iPad (Safari): Open the Remember web address in Safari, tap the Share button (the box with an arrow pointing up), then tap Add to Home Screen and tap Add.

On Android (Chrome): Open the Remember web address in Chrome, tap the three-dot menu, then tap Add to Home Screen.

Opening Remember with Siri (iPhone & iPad)

You can open Remember by voice with a one-time setup that takes about two minutes.

Step 1. Open the Shortcuts app (comes pre-installed — ask Siri "Open Shortcuts" if you can't find it).

Step 2. Tap + to create a new shortcut.

Step 3. Search for and add Open URLs (with an S).

Step 4. Paste this address into the URL field:
https://lynn-edu-apps.github.io/everyday-apps/remember/

Step 5. Name the shortcut Open Remember and tap Done.

From now on say "Hey Siri, Open Remember".

Creating a new note with Siri — asking for name and content

This shortcut asks you for both a note name and the note contents, then creates the note in Remember with the name you gave it.

Step 1. Open Shortcuts and tap +.

Step 2. Add Ask for Input.
Set the prompt to "Note name?" and input type to Text.
This result will be your note name.

Step 3. Add a second Ask for Input.
Set the prompt to "Note contents?" and input type to Text.
This result will be your note contents.

Step 4. Add URL. Type:
https://lynn-edu-apps.github.io/everyday-apps/remember/?action=new&name=
Then insert the NoteName variable from Step 2 (see below for how).
Then type &content= and insert the NoteContent variable from Step 3.

How to insert a variable depends on your iOS version. In recent versions of iOS, after you type an equals sign in the URL (e.g. name=), tap Select variable in the scrollable variable picker bar near the bottom of the screen. After you tap, you will see new items highlighted in blue that say Ask for Input appearing under the steps. Tap on the blue item underneath the step that contains the prompt asking for the value you want (the note name or the note contents).

If this is not consistent with what you see, ask Siri: "How do I insert a variable in Shortcuts".

Step 5. Add Open URL. It uses the URL from Step 4 automatically.

Step 6. Name the shortcut Add a note to Remember and tap Done.

Say "Hey Siri, Add a note to Remember". Siri will ask for the note name, then the contents, then open Remember with the new note ready to view.

Rather than building this shortcut yourself, you can add the ready-made version in one tap:

➕ Add this Shortcut to your iPhone

Tap the link above on your iPhone. iOS will ask you to add it to Shortcuts — tap Add. The shortcut is ready to use immediately.

If you want to build it yourself or understand how it works, the manual steps are described above. Be aware that Shortcuts editing on iOS can be finicky — building it from scratch takes patience.

Having Siri open and speak a note

This shortcut opens Remember, shows a large Speak Note button, and waits for you to tap it. One tap starts reading — no typing or searching needed.

Why a tap? iOS requires one tap to start audio in a browser. The button is large and easy to tap even for users with limited vision or dexterity.

Step 1. Open Shortcuts and tap +.

Step 2. Add URL. Paste:
https://lynn-edu-apps.github.io/everyday-apps/remember/?action=speak&name=Shopping+List
Replace "Shopping+List" with your note name (use + for spaces).

Step 3. Add Open URL. It uses the URL from Step 2 automatically.

Step 4. Name the shortcut Read Shopping List (or whatever you like) and tap Done.

Say "Hey Siri, Read Shopping List". Remember will open, show your note, and display a large Speak Note button. Tap it once to start reading. Tap the Stop button or anywhere on the overlay to stop.

Tip: you can create multiple shortcuts for different notes — one for your Shopping List, one for your Medication Reminders, etc.

Two things to know:

1. Due to browser restrictions, audio cannot start automatically — you must tap the large Speak Note button that appears. This is a one-tap experience by design.

2. The very first time you run the shortcut after clearing your browser history, the overlay may not appear. If this happens, simply run the shortcut a second time — it will work correctly from then on, because the page is now cached on your device.

Adding a new note with Siri (iPhone & iPad)

This lets you say "Hey Siri, Add a note to Remember" and Siri will ask what to write, then open Remember with your words already in a new note.

Step 1. Open Shortcuts and tap +.

Step 2. Add Ask for Input. Set the prompt to "What would you like to note?" and input type to Text.

Step 3. Add URL (not "Open URLs"). Paste:
https://lynn-edu-apps.github.io/everyday-apps/remember/?action=new&content=
The result of Step 2 connects automatically — you should see a dashed line between steps.

Step 4. Add Open URLs. It uses the URL from Step 3 automatically.

Step 5. Name it Add a note to Remember and tap Done.

Note: These instructions are for iOS 26 and later. On older iOS, steps may look different and you may need to manually insert a "Provided Input" variable in Step 3.

Opening Remember with Google Assistant (Android)

Simple (no setup): Say "OK Google, open lynn-edu-apps.github.io/everyday-apps/remember".

Better (one-time setup for a shorter phrase):

Step 1. Open the Google app → profile picture → Settings → Google Assistant → Routines → +.

Step 2. Under "When I say...", type Open Remember.

Step 3. Choose Open website and paste the Remember address. Save.

From now on say "OK Google, Open Remember".

To have Google Assistant read a note aloud, see the "Having Google Assistant read a note aloud" section below.

Having Google Assistant read a note aloud (Android)

This works the same way as the Siri shortcut — Google Assistant opens Remember with a large Speak button, and you tap it once to start reading.

One-time setup:

Step 1. Open the Google app → tap your profile picture → Settings → Google Assistant → Routines → tap +.

Step 2. Under "When I say...", type the phrase you want to use, e.g. Read my shopping list.

Step 3. Under the action, choose Open website and paste:
https://lynn-edu-apps.github.io/everyday-apps/remember/?action=speak&name=Shopping+List
Replace "Shopping+List" with your note name (use + for spaces).

Step 4. Save the routine. Say "Hey Google, Read my shopping list".

Remember will open in Chrome, show your note, and display a large Speak Note button. Tap it once to start reading.

Two things to know:

1. Due to browser restrictions, you must tap the Speak Note button — audio cannot start automatically.

2. The first time you run the routine after clearing your browser history, it may not show the overlay. If this happens, run it a second time — it works correctly once the page is cached.

Tip: create multiple routines for different notes — "Read my medication list", "Read my shopping list", etc.

Adding text to a note by voice with MacroDroid (Android)

Google Assistant Routines can open a note by voice, but they cannot ask you what text to add. For that, you need a free app called MacroDroid.

Simple way — open a specific note by voice (no extra app needed):

Say "Hey Google, open my Shopping List" and set up a Google Assistant Routine that opens:
https://lynn-edu-apps.github.io/everyday-apps/remember/?action=open&name=Shopping+List

Better way — dictate new text into a note:

Step 1. Install MacroDroid from the Play Store (free).

Step 2. Create a new Macro. Set the trigger to Voice Command and type a phrase like Add to Shopping List.

Step 3. Add an action: User Prompt → Input Dialog. Set the prompt to What would you like to add?

Step 4. Add another action: Device Actions → Open URL. Paste:
https://lynn-edu-apps.github.io/everyday-apps/remember/?action=quickadd&name=Shopping+List&content={input_var}
Replace Shopping+List with your note name (use + for spaces).

Step 5. Save the macro. Now say "Hey Google, Add to Shopping List" — MacroDroid will ask what you want to add, then open Remember with your text already inserted into the note.

Android home screen shortcuts (long-press)

If you have installed Remember on your Android home screen as a PWA, you can long-press the Remember icon to see quick action shortcuts:

• New Note — creates a new empty note immediately

• Read Last Note — opens and shows the Speak button for your most recently edited note

• Open Remember — opens the notes list

These shortcuts appear automatically once you have installed Remember as a PWA on Android Chrome. No extra setup needed.

Sharing text into Remember

Remember can receive text shared from other apps (web pages, emails, messages). In any app, tap the Share button and look for Remember in the share sheet — it will create a new note with the shared text.

The share sheet option appears after installing Remember on your home screen.

URL actions (for Siri Shortcuts)

Create a new note:
…/remember/?action=new
…/remember/?action=new&content=Your+text+here
…/remember/?action=new&name=Note+Title&content=Your+text+here
When both name and content are provided, both are required (neither can be blank) and the note name is permanently set — it will not change when you edit the content.

Open a note:
…/remember/?action=open&name=Shopping+List

Open a note and show the Speak button:
…/remember/?action=speak&name=Shopping+List
Opens the note and shows a large full-screen Speak Note button. One tap starts reading aloud. Ideal for use with Siri Shortcuts — see the setup steps in the Optional Setup section above.

Quick-add text to a note:
…/remember/?action=quickadd&content=Your+text+here&name=Shopping+List
…/remember/?action=quickadd&content=Your+text+here&name=Shopping+List&position=2
…/remember/?action=quickadd&content=Your+text+here&name=My+Note&position=after-rule
Appends (or prepends, per your Settings) the content to the named note, then opens it. If the name matches multiple notes, the most recently edited one is used. If no match is found, a new note is created with that name.

Are you sure?
